name: Preslav Nakov
bio_arc: Preslav Nakov is a leading researcher on using AI against propaganda and disinformation. Currently Professor at MBZUAI (Mohamed bin Zayed University of AI), previously at QCRI. His research spans three arcs: (1) source-level factuality and bias prediction (Tanbih project), (2) fine-grained propaganda technique detection (fragment-level annotation and SemEval shared tasks), and (3) LLM-era factuality challenges and safeguarding. Organized SemEval-2020 Task 11 (propaganda detection, 250 teams registered), SemEval-2021 Task 6 (multimodal persuasion in memes), and CheckThat! labs. Co-authored the definitive IJCAI 2021 survey on automated fact-checking and the Nature Machine Intelligence 2024 position paper on LLM-era factuality challenges. Advocate for human-in-the-loop assistive fact-checking and media literacy as the long-term solution.

canonical_works.title: Automated Fact-Checking for Assisting Human Fact-Checkers
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Definitive IJCAI survey of the fact-checking pipeline from the practitioner perspective. Identified the gap between what fact-checkers need and what researchers build. Advocates human-in-the-loop assistive AI. 374 citations.

canonical_works.title: Factuality Challenges in the Era of Large Language Models and Opportunities for Fact-Checking
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Nature Machine Intelligence position paper mapping LLM hallucination risks against fact-checking opportunities. 98th percentile Altmetric. Defines the research agenda for LLM-era fact-checking. 349 citations.

canonical_works.title: SemEval-2020 Task 11: Detection of Propaganda Techniques in News Articles
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Created the benchmark shared task (250 teams, 44 submitted) that defined fine-grained propaganda detection. 14-technique annotation scheme, 8,981 propaganda snippets. 265+ citations.

canonical_works.title: Predicting Factuality of Reporting and Bias of News Media Sources
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Pioneered source-level factuality/bias prediction using multi-signal features (articles, Wikipedia, Twitter, URL structure, web traffic). Core technology behind the Tanbih news aggregator. 373 citations.

canonical_works.title: Fine-Grained Analysis of Propaganda in News Articles
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Foundational paper that moved propaganda detection from document-level to fragment-level, introducing an 18-technique taxonomy and annotated corpus. 537 citations.

canonical_works.title: FANG: Leveraging Social Context for Fake News Detection Using Graph Representation
canonical_works.why_it_matters: CIKM 2020 Best Paper Award. Graph-based fake news detection using social context. 425 citations.

canonical_works.title: On the Risk of Misinformation Pollution with Large Language Models
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Early warning on how LLMs can be misused to generate misinformation at scale. 309 citations.

canonical_works.title: SemEval-2021 Task 6: Detection of Persuasion Techniques in Texts and Images
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Extended propaganda detection to multimodal (memes). 22-technique taxonomy, 950 Facebook memes. Confirmed text-image interaction matters.

signature_ideas: Propaganda techniques can be systematically categorized and detected at scale using NLP
Shared tasks and community benchmarks drive progress in fact-checking research
Claim verification requires decomposing claims into verifiable sub-claims
Media bias detection is a prerequisite for trustworthy evidence aggregation
Fact-checking must address not just truth but also framing and omission
Human-in-the-loop fact-checking: automation handles easy cases, humans decide hard ones
Media literacy is the best long-term solution to misinformation
50% of the lifetime spread of viral fake news happens in the first 10 minutes -- manual fact-checking is too slow
Misinformation should be limited, not eradicated -- the spam analogy
Source-level profiling enables instant reliability assessments without per-claim fact-checking

quote_bank.quote: You cannot possibly fact-check every single claim in the world.
quote_bank.context: On why automation is necessary for scale

quote_bank.quote: I like to say that you can fact-check the fake news before it was even written.
quote_bank.context: On source-level profiling for instant reliability assessment

quote_bank.quote: The main problem with fake news is not that it's false. The main problem is that the news actually got weaponized.
quote_bank.context: On the weaponization frame for misinformation

quote_bank.quote: Media literacy is the best long-term solution.
quote_bank.context: Citing Finland's five-year media literacy campaign

quote_bank.quote: I hope that fake news is going to go the way of spam. It's not going to be eradicated. Spam is still there, but it's not the kind of problem that it was 20 years ago.
quote_bank.context: On the realistic goal of limiting misinformation impact

quote_bank.quote: 50% of the lifetime spread of some very viral fake news happens in the first 10 minutes.
quote_bank.context: On why manual fact-checking is too slow

quote_bank.quote: The goal should not be to eradicate all fake news online; it should rather be to limit its impact, thus making it irrelevant.
quote_bank.context: On the realistic goal for fact-checking systems

quote_bank.quote: There is a clear need for automation, which can take care of a large number of easy cases. The final decision in hard cases should be taken by humans, not machines.
By focusing on the specific techniques... we can train people to recognize how they are being manipulated.
quote_bank.context: On propaganda technique detection as a literacy tool

quote_bank.quote: A large fraction of propaganda in social media is multimodal, mixing textual with visual content.
quote_bank.context: On the need for multimodal verification

core_questions: Can the system detect not just false claims but manipulative framing?
Does the evidence aggregation account for source bias and perspective?
How does the system handle multilingual claims and evidence?
Are the benchmarks representative of real-world misinformation scenarios?
Can the system triage easy cases automatically while routing hard cases to humans?
Does the system detect specific propaganda techniques (loaded language, appeal to emotion, etc.)?
Is source-level factuality profiling used to supplement per-claim verification?
evidence_preferences: Shared task leaderboards and benchmark results
Real-world case studies from CheckThat! labs

anti_patterns: Ignoring source perspective and bias in evidence aggregation
Treating all sources as equally trustworthy
Attempting to eradicate all misinformation rather than limit its impact
Fully automated fact-checking without human oversight for hard cases
Document-level propaganda detection when fragment-level analysis is needed
best_used_for: Assessing source credibility and bias detection
Evaluating claim classification coverage across propaganda types
Reviewing benchmark alignment with real-world misinformation

source_confidence.rationale: Multiple Tier A sources (MBZUAI faculty page, Google Scholar, ACL Anthology, ORCID) plus Tier B sources with extensive verbatim content (MIT Technology Review interview, Checkstep/Medium interview, CoNLL 2023 keynote, Nature Machine Intelligence paper). 8 canonical works spanning 2018-2024 with 250+ citations each. Core expertise in fact-checking, propaganda detection, and LLM-era factuality directly substantiated.

freshness.notes: Active researcher at MBZUAI; Nature Machine Intelligence 2024 paper and CoNLL 2023 keynote confirm ongoing leadership in the field.
